functions forms tissues. Different tissues are organised to form an organ and several organs work together to form organ systems. Such as, nasal pores, nasal cavity, trachea and lungs form respiratory system.

Even when cells are organised into tissues, organs, and organ systems the cell remains the fundamental unit of structure and function in all living organisms. This makes us wonder how such tiny cells perform so many different activities. What are the different components of a cell? How do cells in our body communicate with each other? Do cells live forever, or do they die? In this chapter, we will explore the answers to these questions as we enter the fascinating world of cells!

What do we call the ability of the human eye to see two very close objects as separate and distinct? Imagine two tiny dots drawn on a piece of paper. As the dots are moved closer, there comes a point at which they can no longer be seen as separate. We viewed from about 5 cm the pair of human eye, two points separated by about 0.1 mm can be seen as distinct; otherwise, they appear as a single point. This is called the limit of human eye, which is 0.1 mm.

A cell is equally too small to see, but by the unaided eye (Fig. 2.1). This raises an important question—how have cell biologists studied the structure and function of cells that are much smaller than the limit of resolution of the human eye?

You have learnt about concave and convex lenses. A combination of lenses, i.e., an objective lens and an eyepiece are used for the magnification of an object (Fig. 2.1) to make it appear larger.

Robert Hooke was the first person to observe a cell in 1665 using a self-designed microscope (capable of about 300x magnification). While examining a thin slice of cork, he observed small box-like compartments and named them 'cells'. In school laboratories, light microscopes are used to observe objects using

diagrams.label: Size of Objects and Visibility
diagrams.description: This diagram is a comparative chart illustrating the relative sizes of various objects and biological structures, and how their visibility changes from the unaided eye to magnified views using a light microscope. The diagram is structured vertically with a scale on the left, ranging from 1 km down to 0.1 mm. A horizontal scale is also present, showing distances like 100 m, 10 m, 1 m, 10 cm, 1 cm, etc. The diagram features several labeled objects placed along the scale to demonstrate scale, including a 'Rocket,' a 'Neem Tree,' 'Human height,' and an 'Indian bustard.' The right side shows the 'Unaided eye' view, next to the 'Light microscope' view. Within the magnified section, several biological structures are depicted in cross-section: a rectangular 'Plant cell' (showing a cell wall and chloroplasts), an irregularly shaped 'Animal cell,' and a small, rod-shaped 'Bacteria.' Various organelles are labeled, including the 'Nucleus,' 'Mitochondrion,' 'Chloroplast,' 'Ribosomes,' 'Lipids,' and 'Atoms.' The diagram also includes a scale bar showing the relative size of the objects being studied.

captions: Fig. 2.1: Size of the objects and its visibility through unaided to aided eye
